Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (20) - CSV

Last time we talked a bit about JSON, so this time I want to add a quick extra chapter on handling CSV files. You see CSV all the time when you do bulk imports, so I figured it's worth pulling out into its own post.
When most people first see a CSV file, they kind of assume it's an Excel or Google Sheets thing — which is fair, because that's usually what we use to open them. I used to think CSV
Excel, and I even asked, completely seriously, how you're supposed to open a CSV file.
Maybe I was just being dumb? Or were you like me at first, thinking CSV was just some Excel format? Drop a comment and let me know I'm not alone.
Debating whether I should even admit this

OK, so what actually is CSV? CSV stands for Comma-Separated Values (my English is bad so I find it hard to pronounce). As the name says, you use commas to separate each value, and the first row is usually the column names:
So you can actually get the same result writing it in a .txt file, because a CSV is just a
file. Try creating a plain text file and writing this into it:
Now save it, change the extension to .csv, and open it back up — you'll find it works perfectly. Super easy, right?
So easy the bunny teeth came out
So if you've used any bulk-import service, you'll notice most of them support both .csv and .txt.

So why bother talking about CSV specifically? Like I said, lots of systems support bulk import via .csv. You'll even see crawlers dump their scraped data into CSV and then upload it to some analysis system. So how could you not want to learn how to use CSV in Python?
How could you not learn CSV?
Let's start with reading a CSV as our opening move. I've prepared a simple .csv file with three columns — name, title, and url:

name,title,url
ray,is Ray not Array,https://israynotarray.com/
ray,第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽,https://israynotarray.com/tags/第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽/
ray,第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽,https://israynotarray.com/tags/第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽/
Huh? You're asking where to download the sample .csv? Didn't I just teach you how to create one?
In short, create a .txt text file, paste in the content I gave above, then change the extension to .csv and you're done.
Once you've made it, opening it in Excel or Google Sheets looks like this:
As a side note, you can also drag a .csv file into VSCode and open it there.
But if you find a wall of commas hard to read, you can install the
extension in VSCode to view csv files more nicely:
(Let me sneak in a little product placement here too — if you want to know more handy extensions, you can read
Must-Have Useful Extensions for Visual Studio Code (VSCode)
OK, I got a bit off track. Back to Python. To read a CSV file we need to import the CSV module — same idea as when we used JSON.
But before we import it, let's first try just opening the .csv file:

f = open('example.csv', 'r')
read = f.read()
f.close()
print(read)
This works fine, but if you try to grab a value out of it, you can't:
print(read['name']) # TypeError: string indices must be integers
This is the same situation we had in the JSON chapter.
Oh, and a quick aside — when you're developing in Node.js you also need to import a CSV-related package, something like
So we need to lean on the CSV module's methods to correctly read and convert the .csv data. The reading function is
, and if you want to pull the data out correctly, you have to use a
? The main reason is that when the CSV module processes the data, it treats the file as something you iterate over. What do I mean? Take the earlier example:
Even though I said all that, if you actually print it out you'll see a weird thing:
What on earth is this thing
So I think you can imagine the processed .csv data as looking like this:

[
  ['name', 'title', 'url'],
  ['ray', 'is Ray not Array', 'https://israynotarray.com/'],
  ['ray', '第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽', 'https://israynotarray.com/tags/第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽/']
]
['ray', '第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽', 'https://israynotarray.com/tags/第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽/']
to output the .csv data we just read:

import csv
csvfile = open('example.csv', 'r')

rows = csv.reader(csvfile)
for row in rows:
  print(row)
Look, that fool is calling it easy again
If there's reading, there's obviously writing too, so next we'll try writing data into a .csv.
One of the reasons CSV is so handy is that it's pretty close to an array. As I sort of showed earlier, the shape looks like this:
So the overall concept is very similar to a list, but I'm absolutely not telling you to use
to write to a .csv. Here you still have to use the CSV module's methods. The opening move is to set up the writing behavior, i.e. a
, and you have to tell the CSV module which file you're planning to write to:

import csv
csvfile = open('example.csv', 'r')

csvWriter = csv.writer(csvfile)
Once the opening move is ready, you just use the

import csv
csvfile = open('example.csv', 'w')

csvWriter = csv.writer(csvfile)
csvWriter.writerow(['ray','Fan Page','https://www.facebook.com/israynotarray'])
Now open the file again and you'll see the content was written in successfully:

name,title,url
ray,is Ray not Array,https://israynotarray.com/
ray,第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽,https://israynotarray.com/tags/第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽/
ray,Fan Page,https://www.facebook.com/israynotarray
If you want to write multiple rows, you'll need to pair it with a loop:

import csv
csvfile = open('example.csv', 'w')

csvWriter = csv.writer(csvfile)

data = [
  ['ray', 'is Ray not Array', 'https://israynotarray.com/'],
  ['ray', '第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽', 'https://israynotarray.com/tags/第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽/'],
  ['ray','Fan Page','https://www.facebook.com/israynotarray']
]

for row in data:
  csvWriter.writerow(row)
['ray', '第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽', 'https://israynotarray.com/tags/第-13-屆-iT邦幫忙鐵人賽/'],

open the file and write starting from the end of it

After trying to make Shaoxing drunken chicken today, I found the taste is actually a little different from huadiao wine. Overall huadiao is more fragrant, but the Shaoxing wine flavor is stronger (or maybe I imagined it?). The two taste pretty similar though, and it turned out decent. Speaking of Shaoxing reminds me of a famous fried rice in Qishan, Kaohsiung, called Shaoxing fried rice — once you eat it, it's really hard to forget. If you ever get the chance to visit Qishan, I recommend giving it a try.
